summary: The session focused on the critical values (percentiles) for the $t$ distribution, detailing the significance levels for one-sided and two-sided tests based on degrees of freedom. The user provided a comprehensive table of these values, while the assistant explained the application of the $t$ distribution in hypothesis testing and confidence interval estimation.
facts: The user provided a table of critical values for the $t$ distribution, including percentiles, one-sided significance levels, and two-sided significance levels based on degrees of freedom. The $t$ distribution is used to make inferences about the population mean when the sample size is small and/or the population standard deviation is unknown. The $t$ distribution is bell-shaped and similar to the standard normal distribution but has heavier tails. The shape of the $t$ distribution depends on the degrees of freedom, which is equal to the sample size minus one. The $t$ distribution is used in hypothesis testing to determine if the sample mean is significantly different from a known or hypothesized population mean. A $t$-test is used to calculate a test statistic that is compared to critical values from the $t$ distribution to determine the p-value. The $t$ distribution is also used in confidence interval estimation, which provides a range of values likely to contain the true population mean. As the degrees of freedom increase, the $t$ distribution approaches the standard normal distribution.
